1: וְיַעֲקֹב הָלַךְ לְדַרְכּוֹ (wĕyaʿăqōḇ hālaḵ lĕḏarkô) — "and Jacob went on his way." The phrase lĕḏarkô (on his way/road) — derekh (road, journey, way); the covenant word of the patriarchal narratives: the servant's journey in Genesis 24 was the derekh YHWH prospered; the derekh Jacob vowed to keep safe in Genesis 28:20; now his return derekh begins. וַיִּפְגְּעוּ-בוֹ מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים (and the angels of God met him) — pāgaʿ (to encounter, meet, fall upon — the same verb Jacob's servant used to describe providential meeting in Genesis 24:14; and the wrestling encounter verb in Genesis 32:25). The angels who met Jacob going into exile (Genesis 28:12 — angels on the stairway) now meet him returning from exile.
2: מַחֲנֵה אֱלֹהִים זֶה (maḥănēh ʾĔlōhîm zeh) — "this is the camp of God!" Maḥăneh (camp, encampment) — a military term for an armed force in camp; Jacob names the angelic assembly a divine military camp. וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא מַחֲנָיִם (he called that place Mahanaim) — Maḥănāyim = "two camps" (dual of maḥăneh). The dual name anticipates Jacob's own tactical division of his household into two camps (Genesis 32:7): the divine double-camp inspires the human double-camp strategy. Mahanaim will later become an important administrative center (2 Samuel 2:8; Genesis 17:24; 1 Kings 2:8).
3: וַיִּשְׁלַח יַעֲקֹב מַלְאָכִים לְפָנָיו (wayyišlaḥ yaʿăqōḇ malʾāḵîm lĕpānāyw) — "Jacob sent messengers before him." The word malʾāḵîm (messengers, the same word as "angels") — Jacob sends his own human messengers (malʾāḵîm) to Esau just as God sent divine malʾāḵîm to Jacob (Genesis 32:1). The verbal echo is deliberate: Jacob, who has just encountered divine messengers, now sends his own. ʾEl-ʾaḥîw ʿēśāw ʾarṣāh śēʿîr śĕḏeh ʾĔḏôm (to his brother Esau in the land of Seir, the field of Edom) — the full geographical identification: Esau is in his territory (Seir/Edom, the land whose name connects to his redness and to the hairy goat, Genesis 25:25).
4: כֹּה תֹאמְרוּן לַאדֹנִי לְעֵשָׂו (kōh tōʾmĕrûn laʾḏōnî lĕʿēśāw) — "thus you shall say to my lord Esau." Jacob addresses Esau as ʾăḏōnî (my lord) — the title of social superiority. This is the irony of the blessing: Jacob received the blessing that promised "be lord over your brothers" (Genesis 27:29: geḇîr lĕʾaḥêḵā) and "may the sons of your mother bow down to you" — now Jacob himself uses the submission formula of ʾăḏōnî and calls himself "your servant Jacob" (ʿaḇdĕḵā yaʿăqōḇ). The covenant-heir approaches Esau in the language of a subordinate approaching a superior.
5: The message's content (Genesis 32:5): Jacob catalogues his wealth — cattle, donkeys, sheep, male and female servants — and says he is sending this message "that I may find favor in your eyes." Limṣōʾ-ḥēn bĕʿênêḵā (to find favor in your eyes) — the standard formula of petition to a superior (cf. Joseph finding favor, Genesis 39:4; Moses finding favor, Exodus 33:12-13; Hannah, 1 Samuel 1:18). Jacob is positioning himself as the subordinate seeking favor from Esau — the social reversal of the blessing scene is complete in Jacob's own self-presentation.
6: גַּם הֹלֵךְ לִקְרָאתְךָ וְאַרְבַּע-מֵאוֹת אִישׁ עִמּוֹ — "and also he is coming to meet you, and four hundred men are with him." Four hundred men — a significant military force; the same number appears in 1 Samuel 22:2 (David's initial militia at the cave of Adullam); and in 1 Samuel 25:13 (David's force against Nabal). The number signals a prepared, organized military contingent — Esau is coming with what appears to be a war party. Jacob's fear in Genesis 32:7 is the rational response to this intelligence.
7: וַיִּירָא יַעֲקֹב מְאֹד וַיֵּצֶר לוֹ (wayyîrāʾ yaʿăqōḇ mĕʾōḏ wayyēṣer lô) — "Jacob feared greatly and was distressed." The double emotional expression: yārēʾ (to fear — the existential recognition of threat) + yāṣar (to be narrow, distressed — literally "he was cramped/confined," the claustrophobic experience of being trapped with no way out). Both verbs together describe the complete paralysis of terror. וַיַּחַץ אֶת-הָעָם (wayyaḥaṣ ʾet-hāʿām) — "he divided the people." Ḥāṣāh (to divide, halve — cf. the root of Mahanaim [two camps]: Jacob implements the two-camp strategy suggested by the double-camp of the angels). Strategy emerges from fear: the division of the group into two halves increases the chance that at least one half survives an attack.
8: אִם-יָבוֹא עֵשָׂו אֶל-הַמַּחֲנֶה הָאַחַת וְהִכָּהוּ — "if Esau comes to one camp and attacks it." Jacob's military thinking: if one camp (maḥăneh) falls, the remaining camp (maḥăneh — same word as Mahanaim) escapes. The two-camp strategy is survival-oriented, not victory-oriented: Jacob is planning escape, not defense. The word maḥăneh (camp) appears four times in Genesis 32:7-8, connecting the Mahanaim angel-encounter to Jacob's human response: the divine two-camp (Genesis 32:2) generates the human two-camp (Genesis 32:7-8).
9: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אַבְרָהָם וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי יִצְחָק — "Jacob prayed: O God of my father Abraham and God of my father Isaac." Jacob's prayer (Genesis 32:9-12) is the most developed intercessory prayer in the patriarchal narratives and a model of OT petition: (1) invocation of the covenant God by covenant relationship; (2) appeal to YHWH's own prior promise (Genesis 32:9b: "the LORD who said to me 'return' "); (3) confession of unworthiness (Genesis 32:10); (4) rehearsal of the current situation (Genesis 32:10b: "I have become two camps"); (5) petition for deliverance (Genesis 32:11); (6) appeal to the covenant promise (Genesis 32:12). The structure anticipates the formal prayer patterns of the Psalms (complaint, confession, petition, trust).
10: קָטֹנְתִּי מִכֹּל הַחֲסָדִים וּמִכָּל-הָאֱמֶת (qāṭontî mikkōl haḥăsāḏîm ûmikkol-hāʾemet) — "I am unworthy of all the ḥeseḏ and all the faithfulness." Qāṭan (to be small, too little — in the Qal perfect: I have become small, I count myself small; cf. 1 Samuel 15:17: "when you were small in your own eyes"). Jacob's confession of smallness before divine generosity is the opposite of his earlier self-assertion (Genesis 27: he claimed Esau's identity; Genesis 30: he negotiated shrewdly for the livestock; Genesis 31: he argued his legal case confidently). Here, faced with his brother and his past, he is small. כִּי בְמַקְלִי עָבַרְתִּי (for with only my staff I crossed) — the staff (maqqēl) is the possession of the empty-handed traveler; Jacob departed from Canaan with only a walking stick; he returns with two camps.
11: הַצִּילֵנִי נָא מִיַּד אָחִי מִיַּד עֵשָׂו (haṣṣîlēnî nāʾ miyyaḏ ʾāḥî miyyaḏ ʿēśāw) — "deliver me, please, from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau." The doubled petition — "from the hand of my brother" then "from the hand of Esau" — names both the relational (ʾāḥ, brother) and the personal (Esau) identity of the threat. The doubling emphasizes the relational tragedy: it is his brother from whom Jacob needs deliverance. Kî yārēʾ ʾānōḵî ʾōtô (for I am afraid of him) — the same fear-verb as Genesis 32:7; Jacob brings his fear directly to God as the content of the prayer, not as an embarrassment to confess away.
12: וְאַתָּה אָמַרְתָּ הֵיטֵב אֵיטִיב עִמָּךְ (wĕʾattāh ʾāmartā hêṭēḇ ʾêṭîḇ ʿimmāḵ) — "and you said, I will surely do you good." The emphatic infinitive absolute hêṭēḇ ʾêṭîḇ (I will surely do good) — the unconditional divine promise cited as the basis of the petition: YHWH made a promise; Jacob appeals to it. Wĕśamtî ʾet-zarʿĕḵā kĕḥôl hayyām (and I will make your offspring like the sand of the sea) — the covenant promise of 22:17 and Genesis 28:14; Jacob quotes God's word back to God as the ground for his request. The prayer moves from confession of smallness to citation of divine promise: the two poles of OT petition.
13: Jacob spends the night after the prayer. The narrative pause between prayer (Genesis 32:9-12) and gift-selection (Genesis 32:13-21) is theologically significant: Jacob prays first, then acts. The order signals that the diplomatic strategy of Genesis 32:13-21 is undertaken after and in the context of prayer — it is not a substitute for prayer but a complement to it. The gift-selection is Jacob's human response alongside YHWH's expected divine response.
14: The gift catalogue: 200 female goats, 20 male goats, 200 ewes, 20 rams. The ratios (10:1 female to male) reflect standard ANE flock proportions for productive breeding — the females are the productive units; the males are the minimal breeding complement. Jacob is giving Esau productive, breeding-capable livestock, not just culled animals. The total gift is substantial: 530 animals plus donkeys and camels (Genesis 32:15), approaching the inventory of a wealthy man's flock.
15: The full gift: 30 nursing camels with foals, 40 cows, 10 bulls, 20 female donkeys, 10 male donkeys. The inclusion of nursing camels (gĕmallîm mêyniqôt, nursing camels with their young) shows the thoughtfulness of the gift: these are productive animals with their next generation already present. The total gift spans all the livestock categories Jacob catalogued to Esau's messengers (Genesis 32:5: cattle, donkeys, sheep, goats) — a comprehensive representation of his wealth.
16: עֵדֶר עֵדֶר לְבַדּוֹ (ʿēḏer ʿēḏer lĕḇaddô) — "each herd by itself." The gifts are not sent as one mass but in separate droves with space between them. The spatial arrangement is tactical: each drove arrives separately with its own messenger speaking the same prepared message (Genesis 32:17-19). The effect is a series of encounters: Esau meets one drove, then another, then another — each time receiving the same submissive message ("they are a gift sent to my lord Esau"). The repeated encounters are designed to soften Esau's anger through progressive reconciliation before Jacob himself appears.
17: כִּי-יִפְגָּשְׁךָ עֵשָׂו אָחִי וּשְׁאֵלְךָ לֵאמֹר לְמִי-אַתָּה — "when Esau my brother meets you and asks 'to whom do you belong...?'" The question Esau will ask is the standard challenge-question for a traveling group (lĕmî-ʾattāh, whose are you/what group do you belong to). Jacob prepares his servants with exact scripted answers — the preparation of speech for a diplomatic encounter mirrors the preparation of speech in Genesis 24 (the servant's prayer and sign-request). Both chapters involve carefully planned speech before a decisive meeting.
18: מִנְחָה הִיא שְׁלוּחָה לַאדֹנִי לְעֵשָׂו (minḥāh hîʾ šĕlûḥāh laʾḏōnî lĕʿēśāw) — "it is a gift sent to my lord Esau." Minḥāh (gift, tribute, offering — the same word used for the grain offering in the Levitical system; in diplomatic contexts it is tribute from an inferior to a superior). Jacob sends a minḥāh to Esau — the language of tribute from a subordinate to a lord, consistent with his use of ʾăḏōnî (my lord) throughout the message.
19: The three separate droves carry the same message, delivered by separate servants. The repetition is strategic: Jacob has scripted a consistent story and coached all his servants to deliver it identically. The scripted approach is the opposite of the Laban confrontation, where Jacob's speech was spontaneous legal defense. Here everything is planned, controlled, and aimed at one goal: preventing Esau's anger from becoming violence.
20: כִּי-אֲכַפְּרָה פָנָיו בַּמִּנְחָה הַהֹלֶכֶת לְפָנָי (kî-ʾăḵappĕrāh pānāyw bamminhāh hahōleket lĕpānay) — "I will pacify/cover his face with the gift going before me." Kipper (Piel of kāpar, to cover, atone, appease — the word used throughout Leviticus for the atonement of sin; yôm hakkippurîm, Day of Atonement). Jacob uses the atonement vocabulary: he will "kipper" Esau's face — appease, cover over, propitiate — with gifts. The secular use of kipper for human-to-human appeasement shows that the word's core meaning is to "cover/wipe away" hostility through compensatory action, whether between humans or between humans and God. ʾĂḵappĕrāh pānāyw = "cover his face" — the face (pānîm) that bears the expression of hostility will be covered (its hostility wiped away) by the gift.
21: וַתַּעֲבֹר הַמִּנְחָה עַל-פָּנָיו (wattaʿăḇōr hamminḥāh ʿal-pānāyw) — "the gift passed before him." The gifts go on ahead; Jacob remains in the camp. The word ʾal-pānāyw (before his face) — the gift is literally going "before Esau's face" to prepare the way; the gift-going-before is a form of advance diplomacy. Wĕhûʾ lān ballayĕlāh hahûʾ (he himself spent the night in the camp that night) — Jacob is alone with his plan and his fear before the morning encounter. The next verse will interrupt his night with the wrestling encounter.
22: וַיָּקָם בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא וַיִּקַּח אֶת-שְׁתֵּי נָשָׁיו — "he rose in the night and took his two wives." Jacob's nocturnal action: he rises during the night and sends his family across the Jabbok ford. The sequence is precise: wives → female servants → eleven children → all possessions. ʾēt maʿăḇar yaḇḇōq (the ford of the Jabbok) — Yabbōq is the modern Zarqa river, the northern tributary of the Jordan in Transjordan. The name yaḇḇōq begins with the same three consonants as yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob) — the place name and the person name echo each other in the consonantal text. The wrestling of Jacob (yaʿqōḇ) at the Jabbok (yaḇḇōq) using the verb "wrestle" (yēʾābēq) creates a triple wordplay: yod-ayin-qoph / yod-beth-qoph / yod-aleph-beth-qoph.
23: Jacob sends all his possessions across the ford — the total evacuation of his camp to the other side of the Jabbok. After this verse, Jacob is entirely alone (Genesis 32:24: "Jacob was left alone"). The deliberate stripping away of all family, servants, and goods is Jacob's self-exposure: he crosses over nothing, he has sent everything ahead. The physical aloneness precedes the spiritual encounter.
24: וַיִּוָּתֵר יַעֲקֹב לְבַדּוֹ (wayyiwwātēr yaʿăqōḇ lĕḇaddô) — "Jacob was left alone." Yāthar (Niphal: to be left over, to remain — what remains after everything else has gone). Jacob is the remainder: the last one left behind on his side of the ford. וַיֵּאָבֵק אִישׁ עִמּוֹ עַד עֲלוֹת הַשָּׁחַר (wayyēʾābēq ʾîš ʿimmô ʿaḏ ʿălôt haššāḥar) — "and a man wrestled with him until the breaking of the dawn." ʾĀḇaq (to wrestle/grapple — a hapax in this form; possibly from ʾāḇāq, dust, suggesting rolling in the dust of a wrestling match; or related to a root meaning to clasp, hold). The wrestling partner is identified as ʾîš (a man) — not "angel," not "God" — but the identity is deliberately ambiguous. The narrative withholds the identity; only Jacob's interpretation in Genesis 32:30 ("I have seen God face to face") and Hosea's retroactive identification (Hosea 12:4: "he strove with an angel") name the wrestler after the fact.
25: כִּי לֹא יָכֹל לוֹ (kî lōʾ yāḵōl lô) — "when he saw that he could not prevail against him." The wrestler (ʾîš) is unable to overpower Jacob — the one who claims to be divine (or is) is stalemate against the human. וַיִּגַּע בְּכַף-יְרֵכוֹ (wayyiggaʿ bĕḵap-yĕrēḵô) — "he touched the socket of Jacob's hip." Nāgaʿ (to touch — often of divine touch with transformative effect: Isaiah 6:7 where the seraph touches Isaiah's lips; Jeremiah 1:9 where YHWH touches Jeremiah's mouth; Daniel 10:16, 18). The divine touch on the hip-socket (kap-yāreḵ, the hollow/socket of the thigh joint) dislocates it: watteqaʿ kap-yereḵ yaʿăqōḇ bĕhēʾāḇqô ʿimmô (the socket of Jacob's hip was wrenched as he wrestled). Yāreḵ is the same word as the oath-thigh of 24:2 and Genesis 47:29 — the seat of generative power and oath; here it is the point of divine touch and permanent alteration.
26: שַׁלְּחֵנִי כִּי עָלָה הַשָּׁחַר (šallĕḥēnî kî ʿālāh haššāḥar) — "let me go, for the dawn is breaking." Šālaḥ (to send away, release, let go) — the send-verb that runs through the patriarchal narratives; now Jacob is the one asked to release. The dawn-deadline (kî ʿālāh haššāḥar) is an urgency that has no plain explanation: why must the wrestling end at dawn? Various interpretations: (1) divine beings cannot be seen in daylight; (2) the dawn breaks the theophanic night; (3) the impending day of meeting Esau makes the night's encounter a preparation that must conclude. לֹא אֲשַׁלֵּחֲךָ כִּי אִם-בֵּרַכְתָּנִי (I will not let you go unless you bless me) — Jacob's reply is pure Jacob: he turns the wrestling into a blessing-extraction. From the moment he grabbed Esau's heel at birth to the stolen blessing of Genesis 27, Jacob grasps; now he grasps the divine wrestler for blessing. The irony is that the blessing he extracted through deception from Isaac he now seeks legitimately through struggle with God.
27: מַה-שְּׁמֶךָ (mah-šĕmeḵā) — "what is your name?" The question reverses the pattern of Genesis 27: Isaac asked "who are you, my son?" and Jacob answered falsely ("I am Esau"). Now the divine wrestler asks "what is your name?" and Jacob answers truly: yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob). The name-question in the OT often precedes a name-change (Abram → Abraham, Genesis 17:5). The answer "Jacob" is simultaneously a self-identification and a confession: the supplanter, the heel-grabber, the deceiver — this is who I am. Saying the name truthfully to God may be the first step of the transformation that follows.
28: לֹא יַעֲקֹב יֵאָמֵר שִׁמְךָ כִּי אִם-יִשְׂרָאֵל (lōʾ yaʿăqōḇ yēʾāmer šĕmeḵā kî ʾim-yiśrāʾēl) — "your name shall no longer be Jacob but Israel." The name-change as covenant moment — Abraham (Genesis 17:5), Sarah (Genesis 17:15), and now Jacob. Yiśrāʾēl — etymology in the text: kî-śārîtā ʿim-ʾĔlōhîm wĕʿim-ʾănāšîm wattûḵal (for you have striven with God and with humans and have prevailed). Śārîtā (you have striven/persisted — Qal of śārāh, to persist, exert oneself, strive; possibly related to śar, prince — giving the alternate reading "for you have been a prince with God"). The name Israel = yiśrā (from śārāh) + ʾēl (God) = "he who strives with God" or "God strives/prevails." The wrestling is not Jacob's defeat but his prevailing — wattûḵal (and you have prevailed): Jacob won the wrestling match in some sense, receiving the blessing he demanded.
29: הַגִּידָה-נָּא שְׁמֶךָ (haggîḏāh-nnāʾ šĕmeḵā) — "tell me your name, please." Jacob asks the wrestler's name — a request the wrestler refuses: lāmmāh zeh tišʾal lišmî (why do you ask my name?). The divine refusal to reveal a name is a consistent pattern: in Exodus 3:14 YHWH gives "I AM WHO I AM" — not a proper name but a self-referential formula; in Judges 13:18 the angel tells Manoah "why do you ask my name? It is wonderful/beyond understanding" (wĕhûʾ-pelîʾî). The name of God is not a formula that can be possessed and used; it remains in divine sovereignty. Yet the one who refuses to give his name gives his blessing: wayyĕḇāreḵ ʾōtô šām (he blessed him there). The blessing without the name is itself the covenant: YHWH acts without being controlled by the one who knows his name.
30: וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם פְּנִיאֵל (wayyiqrāʾ yaʿăqōḇ šēm hammāqôm pĕnîʾēl) — "Jacob called the name of the place Peniel." Pĕnîʾēl = "face of God" (pāneh + ʾēl); the place is named from Jacob's experience: כִּי-רָאִיתִי אֱלֹהִים פָּנִים אֶל-פָּנִים (for I have seen God face to face). Pānîm ʾel-pānîm (face to face) — the same expression used for Moses' intimacy with YHWH (Exodus 33:11: "YHWH spoke to Moses face to face as a man speaks to his friend"; Deuteronomy 34:10: "Moses whom YHWH knew face to face"). Jacob's face-to-face encounter with God is the patriarchal precedent for the intimacy that will be named most fully in Moses. וַתִּנָּצֵל נַפְשִׁי (yet my life has been delivered/spared) — the standard formula for surviving a divine encounter that was expected to be fatal (cf. Isaiah 6:5: "I am undone, for I have seen the King, YHWH of armies"; Judges 13:22: "we shall surely die, for we have seen God").
31: וַיִּזְרַח-לוֹ הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ כַּאֲשֶׁר עָבַר אֶת-פְּנוּאֵל (wayyizraḥ-lô haššemeš kaʾăšer ʿāḇar ʾet-pĕnûʾēl) — "the sun rose upon him as he passed Penuel." The sun-rising is both chronological (dawn, as anticipated in Genesis 32:26) and symbolic: Jacob wrestled in darkness and emerges into light. The play on the name Peniel/Penuel (variant spellings) — "face of God" — and the rising sun creates a visual of light where the face of God was. Wĕhûʾ ṣōlēaʿ ʿal-yĕrēḵô (he was limping because of his hip) — the limp is the permanent physical mark of the divine encounter. Jacob walks differently after Peniel: every step for the rest of his life is a reminder of the night he wrestled with God. The blessing and the wound come together from the same encounter.
32: עַל-כֵּן לֹא-יֹאכְלוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת-גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה (ʿal-kēn lōʾ-yōʾḵĕlû ḇĕnê-yiśrāʾēl ʾet-gîḏ hannāšeh) — "therefore the children of Israel do not eat the tendon of the hip socket." The etiological formula ʿal-kēn (therefore, on account of this) closes the Peniel episode with an explanation for a continuing Israelite dietary practice: abstaining from the sciatic nerve (gîd hannāšeh, the sinew of the hollow — possibly the sciatic nerve or the adjacent connective tissue). Gîḏ hannāšeh (gîḏ = sinew, tendon; nāšeh = displaced, moved out of joint — the tendon that moves/is displaced at the joint) — the prohibition is not in the Mosaic law directly but is attested in the Talmud as a halakhic practice derived from this narrative. The closing etiology grounds the community's embodied practice in the patriarchal narrative: every observant Israelite's eating practice is a living memorial to Jacob's night at the Jabbok.
